item.title: "Buried Alive By The National Coal Board" Aberfan Disaster, 21st October 1966
item.description:
"In the dark Welsh valley, On the mountainside, Lay the little children, Close to where they died, Their little lives are ended, Before they reach their goal, Tender little children, Have paid the price for coal." At 9.15 am on Friday, October 21, 1966, a waste tip above the mining village of Aberfan in the valleys of Wales in the UK began to slide down the mountainside, firstly destroying a farm cottage and killing all its occupants. It then approached Pantglas Junior School, where the children had only just returned to their classes after singing All Things Bright and Beautiful at their morning assembly. The slide - containing over 150,000 tonnes of slurry - then engulfed the school and about 20 houses in the village, killing 144 people, including 116 school children and 28 adults.
Workers up in the mountain had seen the slide start, but could not raise the alarm because their telephone cable had been stolen and down in the village, everybody heard the noise, but could see nothing, because of thick fog. News of the tragedy travelled fast and hundreds of people stopped what they were doing and headed to Aberfan to try and help with the rescue. It was futile, as nobody was rescued alive after 11am and it was nearly a week before all the bodies were recovered. On Mynydd Merthyr, directly above Aberfan, several tips containing millions of cubic metres of mining debris from the Merthyr Vale Colliery had been deposited over the years, onto highly porous sandstone that contained numerous underground springs. The NCB's area management had been made aware of the concerns regarding the tipping of spoil above the primary school, but these were largely ignored. In the days leading up to the disaster, there had been substantial bursts of heavy rain, which had caused 3–6 metres of subsidence on one of the tips. This then led to more than 150,000 cubic metres of debris breaking away and flowing downhill at high speed.
The classroom clock is part of the exhibition at Sain Ffagan (The National Museum of Welsh History).
On 26th October 1966, a tribunal was appointed to inquire into the causes of and circumstances relating to the Aberfan disaster, which was chaired by Welsh barrister and Privy Councillor Lord Justice Edmund Davies. The Tribunal's report found that * The blame for the disaster rested entirely with the National Coal Board, and their "total absence of a tipping policy" * Repeated warnings about the dangerous condition of the tip had been ignored. * The tips had never been surveyed and were continuously being added to in a chaotic and unplanned manner. The disregard for the unstable geological conditions and the NCB's failure to act after previous smaller slides were found to have been major factors that contributed to the catastrophe.
Miraculously, some children survived. Seven-year-old Karen Thomas and four other children in the school hall were saved by their brave dinner lady, Nansi Williams, who sacrificed her life by diving on top of them to shield them from the slurry.
The others though, were not as fortunate. As the men worked - the hopes of rescue soon turned to a sombre realisation that all they could do was recover the lifeless bodies of the children. It was a task that scarred many lives - with hundreds of men and women living with the horrific trauma for the rest of their days. Entire generations of children have grown up in the surrounding towns and villages - each of us with a grandfather or father who was there that day or in the following days. Grown men that survived the horrors of war are often moved to tears at the memory of what they witnessed.
There are of course, many tales of unsung heroes of the disaster. On 22 October 1966, the Midland Division of the Institute of Embalmers gathered, bow-tied and ball-gowned, in Nottingham, for the high point of the social calendar – the annual ladies’ night. The banquet was interrupted by a telegram requesting urgent help. In Aberfan, a Welsh village near Merthyr Tydfil, a 40-foot wall of coal waste had slid down a mountain at over 100mph and hit the Pantglas Junior School, killing 116 children and 28 adults. Leaving their partners, the volunteer embalmers returned home to collect equipment, embalming fluid and coffins. Travelling through the night, they arrived in Aberfan to join colleagues from across the UK. Some had flown from Northern Ireland on a plane with the seats removed to accommodate stacks of child-size coffins. Billy Doggart was one of them, and it was he who co-ordinated their extraordinary efforts. Some of the bodies recovered from the school were already wrapped in blankets and laid on the pews of the Bethania Chapel. Makeshift mortuary stations were quickly established. Working without electricity or running water, the embalmers took over from the police and performed their first task: cleaning the bodies for identification. The viscous slurry that had swallowed the school also covered the bodies. One embalmer, fresh from his honeymoon, told me that his first job was to remove a boy’s shirt and take it outside to the waiting parents. He had to hold it aloft and ask whose little boy had been wearing it. Usually in disaster situations such as plane crashes or explosions, identification is a big problem. Not so at Aberfan, where every parent was waiting outside, distraught and eagle-eyed for evidence of their child.
On Thursday, October 27 1966 - 81 of the children and their teachers were laid to rest at the Aberfan cemetery, on a steep hillside overlooking the site of the disaster.
Queen Elizabeth visited the town in the wake of the disaster. It remains to this day one of the few displays of public emotion from the usually stoic monarch as she broke down in tears at the sheer tragedy. Years later in a letter to the village, the Queen said how she was deeply affected by the disaster. She said: I will remember my own visit with Prince Philip after the disaster and the posy I was given by a young girl which bore the heartbreaking inscription: "From the remaining children of Aberfan"
The children were laid to rest in one of the largest mass graves of modern Britain.
item.dc:creator: TheN8

item.title: Motherfucker, please don't.
item.description: I know you follow me here. You've ignored your phone. You left your dog with friends, you left a letter. Look, man, I know things ain't been the same past several years. I found happy times, and for a while, so did you. You had some shit happen, some bad decisions, but goddamn bro ain't that big a deal. Recoverable. I'm sorry we fell out of touch. Please. Don't. Pick up your phone. Check in with a cop. I know you follow me here. I've tried everything else. Please don't be part of the 22.
item.dc:creator: turbodog

item.title: Neurodivergence and trauma.
item.description: Please keep an open mind. There's a lot we don't understand. Nonetheless, I'm glad to see people speaking their mind without fear of judgement. Medical community- on a personal level- what do you truly think of this video?

item.title: Unmasking The People's Monster | Spiritwalker
item.description: Wisconsin-based costume maker Brandon Johnson is best known as Spiritwalker, a sentient four-legged creature with a humanlike face. Using thrifted materials and trash, Johnson handmakes and wears the 9-foot-tall costumes as an outlet to perform, inspire and delight.
item.dc:creator: 60SecondDocs

item.title: I got the job!!!
item.description:
Just screaming into the void here, I was laid off from my company about a month ago because of lack of work. They sent an email to one of their customers who always requested me letting me know I was no longer employed there. The company reached out to me and asked me to apply for a job as their new facilities director, it’s more money, it’s a management roll and I’ll be able to put the tools down. It’s got so much growth potential it’s ridiculous as long as I work hard. I just needed to scream into the void for a moment. I needed this win.
Cat tax, Bella and Flash watching the birds.
item.dc:creator: JustaSimplePlumber

item.title: Not all tears are an evil
item.description:
In tears can be found fortitude, deliverance, and even sanity - this is a lore account on how Tolkien presented this essential expression of ourselves in his legendarium.
Across Tolkien tales people of all kinds and kindreds shed tears, whether in sorrow or joy. This is not a look into all such occasions, but how it is represented as providing release and progress. Thus I begin to note how weeping is not reacted upon as a weakness or embarrassing in the tales; Tolkien - an orphan, a war veteran, and a believer in compassion - was under no such misconception.
The world had been given its shape by the mighty spirits known as the Valar, and mighty among the Valar was Nienna, called the queen of shadow. All the love of Nienna was mingled with pity, grieving for the harms of the world and for the things that fail of fulfilment (art by Haru Herlambang).
Through Nienna was mourning "woven into the themes of the World before it began. But she does not weep for herself; and those who hearken to her learn pity, and endurance in hope" (The Silmarillion; II, art by Leyla Schlopsna).
Nienna had a brother called Mandos, for he was the keeper of Mandos [the Houses of the Dead], to where he summoned the souls of slain Elves. Nienna often came there "and all those who wait in Mandos cry to her, for she brings strength to the spirit and turns sorrow to wisdom" (ibid, art by Rekyrem).
In the First Age the tragic human hero Túrin Turambar unintentionally slew his best friend. Thereafter he would not speak or show any will or emotions for days, until finally "he cast himself down, and his tears were unloosed at last, and he was healed of his madness" (Silmarillion; III; XXI, art by steamy).
The Valar had followers known as the Maiar, uncounted spirits of the same kindred though of less power. Few Maiar are known by name but the wisest was Olórin. He lived in the gardens of a brother and sister-in-law of Nienna, but Olórin's "ways took him often to the house of Nienna, and of her he learned pity and patience" (Silmarillion; II, art by rubinecorvus).
About year 1050 of the Third Age the Valar sent five Maiar to Middle-earth, incarnated as old men, to aid the Free Peoples against the Dark Lord. Among the five emessaries was Olórin, who became known as Gandalf (art by John Howe). In year 3019 Gandalf fell defending the Fellowship of the Ring. The Elves in Lórien sang laments, but Legolas the Elf would not interpret the songs for the Fellowship.
Legolas told them "that he had not the skill, and that for him the grief was still too near, a matter for tears and not yet for song" (The Fellowship of the Ring; II; VII). The companions came to appriciate the fair Lórien and their new friends there. When they finally departed "the sun was before them, and their eyes were dazzled, for all were filled with tears. Gimli wept openly" (FotR; II; VIII, art by annelouisep).
Not long after Boromir of Gondor fell to the lure of the One Ring. Fierce and violently he tried to take the Ring from Frodo Baggins, but Froso escaped while Boromir fell over a stone to the ground (FotR; II; X, art by Anke Eißmann): "For a while he was as still as if his own curse had struck him down; then suddenly he wept. He rose and passed his hand over his eyes, dashing away the tears. 'What have I said?' he cried. `What have I done? Frodo, Frodo!' he called. 'Come back! A madness took me, but it has passed. Come back!'"
Frodo had fled far away, but Boromir redeemed himself in defence against Orcs. Boromir reported to Aragorn what had occured, asked him to go to Gondor, and fell silent (art by Anke Eißmann). Aragorn despaired over the death of Boromir, as well as the ruin of the Fellowship, how he had failed Gandalf's trust to lead them on, and the loss of the Ring and the Bearer. Before he could go on and make decisions he "knelt for a while, bent with weeping, still clasping Boromir's hand. So it was that Legolas and Gimli found him" (The Two Towers; III; I).
Weeks later the armies of Gondor and Rohan marched on Mordor. In Minas Tirith Steward Faramir met Lady Éowyn of Rohan, both who had been wounded in the recent battle. Éowyn was unhappy to remain behind but Faramir noted that they both must endure with patience the hours of waiting (RotK; VI; V, art by Anke Eißmann): "She did not answer, but as he looked at her it seemed to him that something in her softened, as though a bitter frost were yielding at the first faint presage of Spring. A tear sprang in her eye and fell down her cheek, like a glistening rain-drop."
After the Ring was destroyed Sam awoke in a tent. Gandalf asked how he felt, with a laugh that Sam found joyful (The Return of the King; VI; IV, art by Jana Skarecky): "But he himself burst into tears. Then, as a sweet rain will pass down a wind of spring and the sun will shine out the clearer, his tears ceased, and his laughter welled up, and laughing he sprang from his bed. ‘How do I feel?’ he cried. ‘Well, I don’t know how to say it. I feel, I feel’ - he waved his arms in the air - ‘I feel like spring after winter, and sun on the leaves; and like trumpets and harps and all the songs I have ever heard!"
As Gandalf had Olórin ever promoted pity, patience and hope, and when he brought Frodo with him home over the Sea he at end said to Sam, Merry and Pippin: "Well, here at last, dear friends, on the shores of the Sea comes the end of our fellowship in Middle-earth. Go in peace! I will not say: do not weep; for not all tears are an evil." – RotK; VI; IX (art by Ted Nasmith)
For further Tolkien lore posts, here are my indexes: Character bios https://imgur.com/gallery/axV2mPF Folks, animals, items, etc https://imgur.com/gallery/AQK9pJL
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.title: An emotional Sir AnthonyHopkins watches as Andre Rieu's Orchestra plays the Waltz he wrote 50 years previously. Sir Anthony's wife, Stella, contacted Rieu and shared the sheet music.Welsh actor Sir Anthony Hopkins is also a pianist and composer
item.description: An emotional Sir Anthony Hopkins watches as Andre Rieu's Orchestra plays the Waltz he wrote 50 years previously. Sir Anthony's wife, Stella, contacted Rieu and shared the sheet music. Legendary Welsh actor Sir Anthony Hopkins also a pianist and composer with a deep love of Music was invited to the concert, and to his surprise, heard his composition for the first time. The concert was in Vienna.
item.dc:creator: Joyika
